there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the inequality:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

where $ \{x\} $ denotes the fractional part of $ x $. the fractional part of a number is the part after the decimal point, and it is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). this inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of powers of $ x $, starting from $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, and so on, up to $ x^{99} $.

the fractional part of $ x $, denoted $ \{x\} $, is equal to $ x - \lfloor x \rfloor $, where $ \lfloor x \rfloor $ is the greatest integer less than or equal to $ x $. since $ x > 1 $, $ \lfloor x \rfloor $ is at least 2. thus, $ \{x\} $ is between 0 and 1, and it is not an integer.

the inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of odd powers of $ x $, which means $ x, x^3, x^5, \dots, x^{99} $. each of these terms has a fractional part that is between 0 and 1. the sum of these fractional parts is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is a very small number. this implies that the sum of the fractional parts is extremely small, which is only possible if each individual fractional part is extremely small.

since $ x > 1 $, the powers $ x^1, x^3, x^5, \dots, x^{99} $ grow very quickly. for example, $ x^2 $ is already greater than 1, and $ x^3 $ is greater than $ x^2 $. this means that the fractional parts of these powers are not necessarily small, but their sum is still extremely small.
